title: Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita

The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) on the vertical axis and GDP per capita on the horizontal axis for the year 2020. The horizontal axis uses a logarithmic scale to display GDP per capita in international dollars at 2011 prices, ranging from $1,000 to over $100,000. The vertical axis measures the AHDI score from 0 to roughly 0.9. Data points are color-coded by region, with North America in orange, South America in maroon, Africa in purple, Europe in blue, Asia in teal, and Oceania in light blue. Circle sizes correspond to population, with a reference scale indicating sizes for 1.4 billion and 600 million people.

A positive correlation is visible across the dataset, where higher GDP per capita generally aligns with higher AHDI scores. Countries in the lower-left quadrant, such as Burundi, Chad, and the Democratic Republic of Congo, show GDP per capita below $2,000 and AHDI scores between 0.2 and 0.3. Moving toward the center, nations like India, Indonesia, and Egypt cluster around the $5,000 to $10,000 GDP range with AHDI scores spanning roughly 0.3 to 0.5. In the upper-right section, high-income economies including Switzerland, the United States, and Japan exhibit GDP per capita figures exceeding $40,000 and AHDI scores above 0.8. Specific outliers include Qatar, which sits at the far right with a GDP per capita near $100,000 but an AHDI score around 0.4, and Equatorial Guinea, positioned with a GDP near $20,000 and an AHDI score near 0.2. European nations form a dense cluster in the top tier, while African nations are predominantly grouped in the lower-left band.
